item.title: Stars are ending the world, but this time with sun murder!
item.content:encoded: Gotta love world-ending celestial bodies! Stars murdering other stars also happens, cool. +1

item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded: The image is owned by Mark Hillary, under CC BY 2.0.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think that the reasons the Father sun did not kill anything because he loved the creatures he made it so his flames were purely appearances, and the brothers died of the fright of the sun. I imagine the father sun went supernova and something whether it was the Father sun or one of our many many scps that stopped it from hurting us. But this scp, the child of the father sun doesnt care about wether he hurts anyone, the only exception being the children of the flame that tried to free him.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was tarting to read this, and my first impression is I’m very put off by the oddly specific measurements

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So if the facility has FTL ships why not set up an off world site for possable K-CLASS scenario stuff like this?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the implications here-That the Sun is the manifestation of some being's rage at the death of another star, that was killed by other stars that (I might be wrong) became black holes through fear. The idea of stars as living entities is hardly new, but this was wonderful execution and making it into a realistic SCP. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoyed and upvoted the article though this is one of those scips that kinda make me root against the foundation.

The poor world ending star just wants people to love it (and father).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Concept of "world-ending force held in place by magic statues" feels to similar to SCP-319. Execution is good though, so no-vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A little long, but nice writing and a good picture. +1 for good writing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really liked this one, so much that it convinced me to create an account. But I just had a minor question, the final phrase of the addendum reads forced to me. Is it intentional or shouldn't that be "My fires too will scorch the heavens."?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, it seems to have taken awhile, but my +1 means this has hit +100. Congrats!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've liked this one for a while, but some fridge logic got through to me: why is it classified as Keter? The father's rage was harmless.. more or less.. so wouldn't the son's fire also be harmless? Is it a choice? Speaking realistically, a piece of the sun the size of a pinhead could kill all life within 100 miles (The number's less, but I'm not sure by how much.), so I'm sure a miniature sun could still wipe out a good portion of the Earth just by existing. A conscious attack by a sentient, though miniature, sun would be much worse. My question sorted itself out, so never mind.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a great article, but I have a question. Why is it children of the sun and golden wings, instead of children of the sun and wings of gold? "de" translates roughly to of, so if you have "de" in both of their names you should have "of" in both or neither of their english translations fro consistency. Also, you could do "Jīn Chìbǎng" meaning "gold wings" instead. Other than that, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think that was actually the most jarring of the article for me. I know you didn't come up with the GoI's name, but normally occultist (or even religious) group names in Chinese would end with something like "Jiao" (Cult/Religion), "Zong" (Sect) or "Dafa" (Great Way), and the particle "de" as an adjective indicator would always be omitted, and as a possessive would be replaced by the more archaic "zhi".

Also, the proper pinyin is Huoju.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While the description of the item is interesting enough in and of itself, the field report from an Agent utterly tired of "some magic bullshit" got an extra amused upvote from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh man, I supremely love this. Nice work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: On the one hand, it is well-written and neat, on the other, this is like the billionth occult celestial keter entity I've seen and the neatness of its technical aspects and occult connections don't really craft a special connection to it. Kudos on the fine work, but from me, neutral vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This alignment must be upheld at all costs. SCP-2995-1 must maintain a distance from one another of exactly 9.56 meters; to facilitate this, the instances are bound to one another by a series of reinforced steel supports.

Matter will expand or contract when temperature changes due to thermal expansion, and metal is especially susceptible to this, so the temperature of the containment chamber would have to be kept constant. Though it would probably be better to make the connecting supports out of a material that undergoes little thermal expansion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Whee more East Asian SCPs! Kind of bothered by anachronistic slang in an incident report from 1937, but otherwise I like this a lot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Actually, I should be mad at you because I had an idea for skip that involved a cult setting themselves on fire, and I think it would both rub up against this article too much and not add anything to the HdH narrative, so I'll likely not be able to do it. :P But that's not a big loss.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Another solid piece in the series that expands and adds to the overall story. Nicely done.

My only issue is that the incident report doesn't quite strike me as being written in 1937. I'm not necessarily saying that the guy should be saying "the bee's knees" repeatedly or complaining about FDR, but the dialogue just feels kind…off, I suppose. Not sure how I would fix it, and it wasn't enough to affect my vote, but it did break immersion a little bit.

On another note, in addition to the good storytelling, I appreciate this series for adding more international viewpoints to the material on the site, something I feel we could use a bit more of.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wonderful job on the physics, as well as the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If anyone wants to write for Huoju de Haizi, I welcome it. I had originally only intended on making a series, not a full blown GoI, though I permit other authors to use HdH.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was wondering if this was another Houju de Haizi, and I'm pleased to see it. The first log is kind of dramatic and grimdark, but the second is great and really expands on this GoI, which I assume it is now. It just tells a really great story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I like this one a lot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Special thanks to Riemann for giving the physics of this article a good scrub.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That is some good physics. Yessirree.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it. A star trapped by magic.

Also I am looking forward to more Children of the Torch stories. Can't wait until this becomes a new GOI.

If you like it on chat, why not like it here?

Answer: there would be no reason not to like it. Furthermore, not liking it would be silly. And I'm not that silly.

Sillyness asides, upvoted due to the notion of an anomalous group trying to get to a cosmic entity trapped by the Foundation by fire and goooood procedures. :D
